---
title: "Vignette - Working with Python and Chatlas"
id: vignette_working_with_python_and_chatlas
author: brad_cannell
date: 2026-01-28
date-modified: 2026-01-28
audience: developers
category: vignette
tags:
  - ai
  - python
  - chatlas
  - wiki
  - sop
  - knowledge_base
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  eval: false
---

# Overview

This vignette provides information about using Python and Chatlas](https://posit-dev.github.io/chatlas/) to interact with AI assistants.

# Create a virtual environment (venv)

See `vignettes/UV Virtual Environments.qmd`

# Install Packages

You can't just run `pip install [package]` because it won't install to the correct version of Python. Instead, you can run the code below to get the path to install the package on the version of Python Positron is using.

You still need to open a terminal window to install.

If using a virtual environment (.venv), before you can install or use packages in your venv, you must activate it in the terminal with: `source ../.venv/bin/activate`

::: callout-note
If the virtual environment isn't working, try:
- Opening the the Command Palette: `Cmd+Shift+P` (Mac).
- Type and select Python: Create Environment.
- Select venv as the provider.
- Select the Python interpreter you want to use. 
- In the terminal, type `source ../.venv/bin/activate`.
:::

You might need to restart the kernel after you install the package.
- To restart the Python kernal, open the `Console` pain, select the kernal you want to restart on right, click the circular arrow button.

```{python}
import sys
print(sys.executable) # Path the Python interpreter that is actually running the code. 
print(sys.version) # Python interpreter version
```

```bash
which pip # Pip that will be used to install Python packages
```

If the `venv` is activated, you should be able to run `pip install` in the terminal normally, and the packages should be installed into the virtual environment.

```bash
#| eval: false
pip install keyring
pip install openai
pip install jupyter
pip install -U chatlas
```

## Pip Freeze

```bash
pip freeze
```

The code above prints every Python package installed in the current environment with exact versions. The output is a snapshot of your virtual environment at that moment in time.

### Why this matters

Python packages change **constantly.**

pip freeze lets you say:

> “This exact combination of packages is known to work.”

That’s gold for:
- Reproducibility
- Debugging
- Future-you
- Collaborators
- Deployments

### The canonical use: requirements.txt

Optional best practice (recommended)

Most commonly, you do:

```bash
pip freeze > requirements.txt
```

This creates `requirements.txt` at the project root.

Now, you can the environment from scratch:

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

That gives you:
- Reproducibility
- Easy rebuilds
- Fewer “why did this break?” moments

Positron + Quarto + APIs + notebooks really benefit from this.

# Load Packages

```{python}
import os
import keyring
from openai import OpenAI
import sys
import json
from pathlib import Path
import chatlas as ctl
from chatlas import ChatOpenAI
from datetime import date
import requests
```

# File Paths and Environment Variables

Optional, but I like saving file paths and enivronment variables near the top of the conversation. It makes the conversations easier to reuse in the future. 

- Set today's date.
- Set files paths.
    - Especially when using prompt templates
- Set full path to file content that will be passed to the AI assistant.
- Set the model to inject into the API calls. See available models at: https://platform.openai.com/docs/models.

They are passed to the AI assistant below.

```{python}
# Set today's date
today = date.today().isoformat()

# Set file paths (optional)
template_prompt_path = Path("prompts/template_prompt.md")

# Files (optional)
template_prompt_content = template_prompt_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")

# Model
model = "gpt-5-nano-2025-08-07"
```

# Load helper functions

[ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com/g/g-p-694c5854fc0481918223d2c7988b756b/c/69730d63-d5bc-8333-8273-e13d8b6b4296) help with setting this up.

Run in terminal:
- `cd "/Users/bradcannell/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-brad.cannell@epi-workbench.com/My Drive/AI Assistant/py_scripts"`
- `python -m pip install -e .`
- Restart kernal

```{python}
# Load functions from py_scripts/ewb_py_scripts
from ewb_py_scripts import (
    prompt_extract_system_prompt,
    prompt_extract_user_message,
    prompt_fill_placeholders,
)
```

# Expose API

This assumes you've already created an API key on [openai's website](https://platform.openai.com/api-keys), and that you saved the API key in keyring using `keyring.set_password("API Name", "User", "API Key")`.

```{python}
# Retrieve the password from the system keyring
openai_api = keyring.get_password("bradGPT", "brad")

if openai_api is not None:
    # Set the retrieved value as a temporary environment variable
    os.environ['OPENAI_API_KEY'] = openai_api
    print("Environment variable 'OPENAI_API_KEY' set successfully within the script.")
else:
    print("Could not retrieve password from keyring.")
```

# Read the Template Prompt

```{python}
# Raw system (developer) section of the prompt template.
system_prompt = prompt_extract_system_prompt(template_prompt_path)

# Raw user message section of the prompt template.
user_message_raw = prompt_extract_user_message(template_prompt_path)

# Inject course and lesson name into user message (optional)
user_message = prompt_fill_placeholders(
    text = user_message_raw,
    replacements = {
        "lesson_name": lesson_name,
        "course_name": course_name,
    },
)
```

# Pass the Template Prompt to the AI Assistant

https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering?lang=python#message-roles-and-instruction-following

```{python}
client = OpenAI()

chat = client.responses.create(
    model = model,
    instructions = system_prompt,
    input = user_message
)
```

## Response

Review the response, but there typically isn't a good reason to save it. It's just some version of ChatGPT repeating back the instructions from the first prompt.

```{python}
print(chat.output_text)
```

# Pass Text File Contents to the AI Assistant

```{python}
#| echo: false
chat.chat(file_content, echo='none')
```

# Write the Revised Lesson Text to a Markdown File for Review

```{python}
(folder_path / ("file_name" + today + ".md")).write_text(
    str(chat.get_last_turn()),
    encoding='utf-8'
)
```

# Follow-up and Refinement

A section for optional follow-up questions and refinements. 
- Optionally, create a fork of the chat. `import copy; chat_fork = copy.deepcopy(chat)`
- Asks follow-up questions in interactive mode - `chat.app()`.

## Section - Summary

Optionally, ask for a lesson summary.

```{python}
prompt_summary = "Please write a brief summary paragraph to wrap up this lesson."
chat.chat(prompt_summary, echo='none')
```

### Response

```{python}
#| echo: false
print(chat)
```

# Save Chat History

Optional, but necessary to return to the conversation later.

```{python}
# Export chat history as a markdown file
# This version is easier to pass back to to the chat object in the future.
chat_history_path_md = (lesson_folder_path / "ai" / ("lesson_revisions_history_" + today + ".md"))
chat.export(chat_history_path_md, overwrite=True)

# Export chat history as an HTML file
chat_history_path_html = (lesson_folder_path / "ai" / ("lesson_revisions_history_" + today + ".html"))
chat.export(chat_history_path_html, overwrite=True)

# Export chat history as a json file
# Useful for saving/loading the history to/from a database or file.
# chat_history_path_json = (lesson_folder_path / "ai" / ("lesson_revisions_history_" + today + ".json"))
# with open(chat_history_path_json, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
#     json.dump(
#         [json.loads(x) for x in turns_json],
#         f,
#         indent=2,
#         ensure_ascii=False
#     )
```

## Condense

You can also optionally condense the history by asking the LLM to summarize it. This is useful if you want to keep the context of the conversation, but don’t want to provide the entire history to the model on each new submission.

```{python}
# chat.chat("Can you summarize our conversation so far?")
# chat.set_turns([chat.get_last_turn()])
```

## Load chat history

To optionally load the chat history later.

```{python}
# Import chat history as a md file
# chat_history_path_md = (lesson_folder_path / "ai" / ("lesson_revisions_history_" + "yyyy-mm-dd" + ".md"))
# history_md = chat_history_path_md.read_text(encoding="utf-8")

# Import chat history as a json file
# chat_history_path_json = (lesson_folder_path / "ai" / ("lesson_revisions_history_" + "yyyy-mm-dd" + ".json"))
# history_json = json.loads(chat_history_path_json.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
```

# Render to HTML

Render to HTML (Optional)
- Click the Preview button.
- Type `quarto render "path/Lesson Revisions.qmd"` in the terminal.

# Pass File to the API

Above, we did the following to pass the Qurto lesson file (`Measures of Dispersion.qmd`) to the AI assistant:
- Read in the contents of the lesson file as plain text.
- Pass to lesson text to the AI assistant as plain text.

This works, but there may be times when we want to pass the entire file. Here are some instructions for doing so: 
 - https://platform.openai.com/docs/quickstart#analyze-images-and-files
 - https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/pdf-files

 ::: callout-important
 Only the following file formats are currently supported: .art, .bat, .brf, .c, .cls, .css, .diff, .eml, .es, .h, .hs, .htm, .html, .ics, .ifb, .java, .js, .json, .ksh, .ltx, .mail, .markdown, .md, .mht, .mhtml, .mjs, .nws, .patch, .pdf, .pl, .pm, .pot, .py, .rst, .scala, .sh, .shtml, .srt, .sty, .tex, .text, .txt, .vcf, .vtt, .xml, .yaml, .yml.

 **Quarto is not one of them**. Do this instead:
 - Save a copy of the Quarto file.
 - Change the file extension from `.qmd` to `.md`.

 **Markdown doesn't work either**.
 As of 2026-01-24, the code below was returning an error telling me to try uploading a PDF file. The [best solutions](https://chatgpt.com/g/g-p-694c5854fc0481918223d2c7988b756b-openai-api-python/c/69752442-ee64-832f-bf7e-95907c5cdbbd) I could find were:
 1. Pass the contents as text. (This was our original approach)
 2. Convert the markdown to PDF.
 3. Add the markdown file to a vector store. Probably overkill for most scenarios. This is really meant for a knowledge base.
 :::

 ```{python}
 # Upload a file and use it as input
 file = client.files.create(
    file = open("EWB_Tests/Measures of Dispersion Markdown Copy.md", "rb"),
    purpose = "user_data"
)
 ```

 ```{python}
 # Use the uploaded file as input
 chat_with_file = client.responses.create(
    model = model,
    input = [
        {
            "role": "user", 
            "content": [
                {
                    "type": "input_file",
                    "file_id": file.id,
                },
                {
                    "type": "input_text",
                    "text": "Please summarize the main point of this file.",
                }   
            ]
        }
    ]
 )
 ```

## Response

```{python}
#| echo: false
print(chat_with_file.output_text)
```

# Vector Stores

- [ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com/g/g-p-694c5854fc0481918223d2c7988b756b-openai-api-python/c/69753f22-1270-8326-b766-b1f07e727a22) chat about this.
- [OpenAI Default Vector Store](https://platform.openai.com/storage/files/file-QZHxdLoigGckm88dJf4mvU)

Test:
- I have a Word document on my computer's desktop. Here is the path: `'/Users/bradcannell/Desktop/About CND.docx'`. 
- I want to add that file to a vector store (I'm fine with doing it through the browser). 
- I want to use that document in a chatlas chat where I ask, "who are the members of the leadership team at the TCU Center for Neurodegenerative Disease?"

Steps:
1. Open the OpenAI developer dashboard.
2. Create a vector store.
3. Upload the files into that vector store.
4. Wait for indexing to finish.
5. Use that vector store from chatlas `{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": ["<vector_store_id>"]}`.
6. Create a chatlas chat client (see below).
7. Ask your question with file_search enabled (see below).


```{python}
from chatlas import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(
    model=model
)

vs_id = "vs_6972fd3c48308191800b7e1b42c55daa"

resp = chat.chat(
    "Using ONLY the uploaded document, list the leadership team members for the TCU Center for Neurodegenerative Disease. "
    "Return as bullets in the format: Name — Title. If the document does not explicitly say, write 'Not stated'.",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": [vs_id]}]
    }
)
print(str(resp))
```

This worked!

### Things to keep in mind:

- Right now, **the tools need to be provided on the request(s) where you want retrieval.** If you want file_search available for _multiple turns_, just keep passing the same `kwargs={"tools":[...]}` on each `chat.chat(...)` call (or set it once via `chat.set_tools(...)` if you prefer managing tools at the chat object level).

- **The vector store isn't free.** Currently, it costs $0.10 / GB of vector storage per day (first GB free). 
  - Source: https://openai.com/api/pricing/

# Using Websites

- [ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com/g/g-p-694c5854fc0481918223d2c7988b756b-openai-api-python/c/69753f22-1270-8326-b766-b1f07e727a22) chat about this.

Test:
- I want to use this website as context for a model: https://harriscollege.tcu.edu/faculty-staff/view/michelle-kimzey.
- And then I want use that website as context in a chatlas chat where I ask, "what courses does Michelle Kimzey teach at TCU?"

## Method 1: Add the website to a vector store (browser)

Steps:
1. Open the page in a browser.
2. Save it as a PDF.
3. Upload PDF to your vector store.
4. Ask chatlas using that website as context `(file_search)`.
5. Make it extra strict (recommended).
  - This reduces “hallucinated courses” by forcing the model to stick to retrieved text.
6. (Optional) Use `"include": ["file_search_call.action.sources"],` to ask for sources so you can verify what it used.

```{python}
from chatlas import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(
    model=model
)

vs_id = "vs_6972fd3c48308191800b7e1b42c55daa"

resp = chat.chat(
    "Using ONLY the uploaded page content, list the courses Michelle Kimzey teaches at TCU. "
    "Return bullet points exactly as shown on the page.",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "file_search", "vector_store_ids": [vs_id]}],
        # Optional
        "include": ["file_search_call.action.sources"],
    }
)
print(str(resp))
```

This worked!

```` markdown
• HCHS 30783/NURS 30433: Families, Caregivers, and People                                                                       
• NURS 30330: Dementia in the Community                                                                                         
• NURS 40871: Clinical Reasoning & Simulation III: Lab   
````

### Pros
- This is the simplest reliable method.
- Deterministic
- Reusable
- Indexed once, queried many times
- Works perfectly with vector stores
- Easy to explain to collaborators
### Cons (important)
- Manual step

## Method 2: Live Search

Steps:
1. Create a chatlas chat.
2. Ask the question, with web_search enabled.
 - This version uses your exact URL as a “target” and asks the model to rely on it.
3. Make it more deterministic with domain filtering (recommended)
  - OpenAI supports domain allow-lists for web search via `filters.allowed_domains`. This reduces the chance the model grabs a different site that mentions her.
4. (Optional) Ask for sources so you can verify what it used.
  - OpenAI’s docs show you can request the full list of consulted URLs via `include=["web_search_call.action.sources"]`.

```{python}
from chatlas import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(
    model=model
)

url = "https://harriscollege.tcu.edu/faculty-staff/view/michelle-kimzey"

resp = chat.chat(
    f"Use web search to open and read this specific page:\n{url}\n\n"
    "Question: What courses does Michelle Kimzey teach at TCU?\n\n"
    "Answer with a bulleted list of the course titles (and course numbers if shown).",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "tool_choice": "auto",
    },
)

print(str(resp))
```

This worked!

```` markdown
Michelle Kimzey teaches the following courses at TCU:                                                                            

 • HCHS 30783/NURS 30433: Families, Caregivers, and People                                                                       
 • NURS 30330: Dementia in the Community                                                                                         
 • NURS 40871: Clinical Reasoning & Simulation III: Lab   
````

```{python}
# 3. Make it more deterministic with domain filtering (recommended)

from chatlas import ChatOpenAI

chat = ChatOpenAI(
    model=model
)

resp = chat.chat(
    f"Use web search and rely on this page for the answer:\n{url}\n\n"
    "What courses does Michelle Kimzey teach at TCU?",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [
            {
                "type": "web_search",
                "filters": {
                    "allowed_domains": ["harriscollege.tcu.edu", "tcu.edu"]
                },
            }
        ],
        "tool_choice": "auto",
    },
)

print(str(resp))
```

This worked!

```` markdown
Michelle Kimzey teaches the following courses at TCU:                                                                            

 • HCHS 30783/NURS 30433: Families, Caregivers, and People                                                                       
 • NURS 30330: Dementia in the Community                                                                                         
 • NURS 40871: Clinical Reasoning & Simulation III: Lab   
````

```{python}
# 4. (Optional) Ask for sources so you can verify what it used.

resp = chat.chat(
    f"Use web search to answer from this page:\n{url}\n\n"
    "What courses does Michelle Kimzey teach at TCU?",
    kwargs={
        "tools": [{"type": "web_search"}],
        "tool_choice": "auto",
        "include": ["web_search_call.action.sources"],
    },
)

print(str(resp))
```

This worked!

```` markdown
Michelle Kimzey teaches the following courses at TCU:                                                                            

 • HCHS 30783 / NURS 30433: Families, Caregivers, and People                                                                     
 • NURS 30330: Dementia in the Community                                                                                         
 • NURS 40871: Clinical Reasoning & Simulation III: Lab                                                                          

Source: Michelle Kimzey's profile page (Courses Taught section). (tcu.edu)       
````

### Things to keep in mind:

- If the model ever answers without actually searching (it can happen), change the prompt to: “Use web search now. Do not answer until the page has been opened.” …and keep the domain filter on.

- **The web search tool isn't free.** Currently, it up to $25 per 1K calls. 
  - Source: https://openai.com/api/pricing/

### Pros
- Fast
- No preprocessing
- Good for one-off factual questions
### Cons (important)
- Not deterministic (page may change)
- Not reusable across turns
- Not cached for future chats
- Not guaranteed the model actually reads the page
- Not ideal for course / research workflows

# Reusable Online Prompts

- Create the prompt online at Dashboard > Chat.
- After publishing the prompt, Openai will show the prompt id (e.g., pmpt_6975293e2a6481949b7204ca335b6a400130bbd9a3c8bda0) and sample code for using the prompt.

```{python}
response = client.responses.create(
  prompt={
    "id": "pmpt_6975293e2a6481949b7204ca335b6a400130bbd9a3c8bda0",
    "version": "1",
    "variables": {
      "lesson_name": "Measures of Dispersion",
      "course_name": "Introduction to Descriptive Analysis in R"
    }
  }
)

print(response.output_text)
```

```markdown
Of course! Please go ahead and share your excerpts—I'll review them and offer suggestions for clarity, engagement, and instructional effectiveness, as well as any opportunities to better align with your course objectives or integrate R examples if appropriate.
```

I can imagine how that would be useful.
- It would cut down on the amount of code we would need to submit.
- It might cut down on the number of tokens we use.

Concerns
- Can't use all models unless you verify your organization.
- I'm not sure how to build on the chat.

## Follow-up messages

[ChatGPT](https://chatgpt.com/g/g-p-694c5854fc0481918223d2c7988b756b-openai-api-python/c/69753f22-1270-8326-b766-b1f07e727a22) chat about this.

```{python}
response_w_follow_up = client.responses.create(
  prompt={
    "id": "pmpt_6975293e2a6481949b7204ca335b6a400130bbd9a3c8bda0",
    "version": "1",
    "variables": {
      "lesson_name": "Measures of Dispersion",
      "course_name": "Introduction to Descriptive Analysis in R"
    }
  },
  input=[{"role": "user", "content": lesson_content}],
)
```

```{python}
print(response_w_follow_up.output_text)
```

This seems to have worked.
